claim: Major languages (C, C++, Java, JavaScript, Rust, Swift, Go) converge on the DRF-SC guarantee: data-race-free programs behave sequentially consistently, with synchronization operations defining happens-before.
positive_scope: Evidence that DRF-SC is the shared baseline contract an optimizer-first language would likely need to state explicitly.
negative_scope: Does not decide how racy programs should behave (UB vs bounded); survey is a secondary source.

claim: Languages split on race semantics: C/C++/Rust/Swift make data races undefined behavior, while Java/JavaScript/Go bound racy outcomes (Java for security, Go by explicitly constraining implementations and allowing race-detection halts).
positive_scope: Documents the main tradeoff: UB races give optimizers maximal freedom; bounded races constrain optimizations but limit blast radius.
negative_scope: No performance quantification of the difference; the right choice for a new language is an open debate question.

claim: Memory models restrict classic optimizations on possibly-shared memory: compilers must not introduce writes on paths that would not store, must not let one read observe multiple values (re-loading), must not cache volatile/atomic values in registers across iterations, and must respect acquire/release fences as motion barriers.
positive_scope: Concrete list of optimizer freedoms lost when memory may be shared — directly relevant to how much a language should let the type system prove non-sharing.
negative_scope: Rules differ per language/IR; LLVM IR semantics (undef racing loads) are not identical to source models.

claim: Rust demonstrates a type-system route to data-race freedom: Send/Sync auto traits plus exclusive &mut borrowing prevent data races in safe code, so the optimizer can treat non-atomic memory as unshared unless types say otherwise.
positive_scope: Evidence that sharing can be a type-level fact, complementing the aliasing findings (F001).
negative_scope: Unsafe code and incorrect manual Send/Sync impls are UB; does not eliminate the need for an atomics model. Amended 2026-07-02: data-race prevention is authoritatively stated in nomicon/races.html (originally cited send-and-sync page leaves the link as a TODO); safe Rust prevents data races but NOT general race conditions, which cannot violate memory safety alone.

claim: LLVM grades atomic orderings from NotAtomic through SequentiallyConsistent, each enabling/forbidding specific transformations (splitting, narrowing, rematerialization, hoisting, DSE), and treats racing non-atomic loads as undef rather than instant UB.
positive_scope: Shows the IR vocabulary an optimizer-first language must target; source-level ordering choices map to concrete pass freedoms.
negative_scope: IR contracts require correct frontend lowering; pass-specific legality varies.

claim: Java final fields give initialization safety (readers see final field values without synchronization) only when the reference does not escape construction — an example of a language carving out a narrow, optimizer-compatible publication guarantee.
positive_scope: Evidence for immutability-after-construction as a publication contract.
negative_scope: FAQ-level source; escape analysis and constructor discipline are preconditions.
